article.title: Oral PCSK9 Inhibitor Enlicitide Cuts LDL Cholesterol by 60 Percent in Phase 3 Trials, Challenging Injectable Dominance
article.summary: Merck's once-daily pill enlicitide decanoate matched injectable PCSK9 antibodies across two Phase 3 trials, reducing LDL cholesterol by up to 58.2 percent with a safety profile comparable to placebo.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Merck's investigational oral PCSK9 inhibitor enlicitide decanoate has achieved cholesterol reductions previously possible only with injectable therapies, according to results from two Phase 3 trials in the CORALreef clinical program. In the CORALreef Lipids trial, which enrolled 2,909 adults with or at risk for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, enlicitide lowered LDL cholesterol by 57.1 percent at 24 weeks compared with a 3.0 percent increase in the placebo group, as [published in The New England Journal of Medicine](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2511002). A separate trial in patients with hereditary high cholesterol showed a 58.2 percent reduction in the enlicitide group at the same timepoint. If approved, enlicitide would become the first orally administered PCSK9 inhibitor available to patients, a development that researchers say could reshape how high cholesterol is managed worldwide.

## The CORALreef Lipids Trial

The CORALreef Lipids study randomized participants across 168 sites in 14 countries in a 2:1 ratio to receive either 20 mg of enlicitide once daily or placebo for 52 weeks, according to the [NEJM publication](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2511002). Participants had a mean age of 63 years, 40 percent were women, and 97 percent were already taking statin therapy. The mean baseline LDL cholesterol level was 96.1 mg/dL, indicating that many patients remained above recommended targets despite existing treatment.

At 24 weeks, the primary endpoint, enlicitide achieved an adjusted between-group LDL cholesterol reduction of 55.8 percentage points (p<0.001). By 52 weeks, the mean reduction in LDL cholesterol was 47.6 percent, as reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260321012659.htm). Among patients receiving the drug, 70.3 percent achieved LDL cholesterol below 70 mg/dL with at least a 50 percent reduction from baseline, compared with 1.5 percent in the placebo group.

The drug also lowered several other lipid markers linked to cardiovascular risk. Non-HDL cholesterol fell by 53.4 percent, apolipoprotein B by 50.3 percent, and lipoprotein(a) by 28.2 percent, all statistically significant compared with placebo, according to the [NEJM data](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2511002).

## The CORALreef HeFH Trial

A second Phase 3 study, CORALreef HeFH, evaluated enlicitide specifically in 303 adults with heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, a genetic condition that causes dangerously elevated cholesterol from birth. This trial demonstrated a 58.2 percent LDL cholesterol reduction in the enlicitide group at week 24, with a between-group difference of 59.4 percentage points compared with placebo, [according to results published in the Journal of the American Medical Association](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41206969/).

At week 52, the enlicitide group's LDL reduction was 55.3 percent, slightly attenuated from the 24-week result, while the between-group difference widened to 61.5 percentage points as placebo LDL levels drifted upward, according to [JAMA](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41206969/). Dosing compliance was 96 percent and intervention compliance reached 97 percent, suggesting strong patient adherence to the once-daily oral regimen.

## Safety Profile

Across both trials, adverse event rates were comparable between enlicitide and placebo. In the CORALreef Lipids study, adverse events occurred in 64 percent of patients on enlicitide versus 62 percent on placebo, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260321012659.htm). Serious adverse events were reported in 10 percent of the enlicitide group and 12 percent of the placebo group. Discontinuations due to adverse events were also similar at 3 percent versus 4 percent. In the HeFH trial, discontinuations due to adverse events were 2.0 percent for enlicitide versus 3.0 percent for placebo, according to [JAMA](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41206969/). New-onset diabetes rates and mortality were identical between the groups in both studies.

## How Enlicitide Works

PCSK9 is a protein that degrades LDL receptors on the surface of liver cells. With fewer receptors available, the body removes less LDL cholesterol from the bloodstream. Injectable PCSK9 inhibitors such as evolocumab and alirocumab, which are monoclonal antibodies, have been available since 2015 and reduce LDL cholesterol by approximately 60 percent. However, their use has been limited by patient reluctance toward injections, high costs, and supply constraints, as noted by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260321012659.htm).

Enlicitide is a small-molecule macrocyclic peptide that blocks PCSK9 from binding to LDL receptors through oral administration. Dr. Ann Marie Navar, a researcher involved in the trial, stated that the cholesterol reductions achieved by enlicitide "are the most we have ever achieved with an oral drug by far since the development of statins," according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260321012659.htm).

## What Remains Unknown

While the lipid-lowering data are robust, the critical question of whether enlicitide actually prevents heart attacks and strokes has not yet been answered. The CORALreef Outcomes trial, a cardiovascular outcomes study that has completed enrollment with more than 14,500 participants, is designed to address this question but results are not yet available, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260321012659.htm).

Merck has stated that it plans to share data from three pivotal Phase 3 studies with regulatory authorities worldwide but has not yet submitted a formal application for approval to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. The company has not disclosed a target date for regulatory submission.

Fewer than half of patients with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease currently reach their LDL cholesterol goals, according to Dr. Navar, as reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260321012659.htm). An oral option that matches injectable efficacy could help close that treatment gap, though pricing, insurance coverage, and the pending cardiovascular outcomes data will ultimately determine the drug's clinical impact.
